questions.question: Explain the difference between sequential access and direct (random) access methods for files. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Sequential access reads/writes records in order, one after another, like tape; pointer moves only forward
Direct access allows positioning to any record directly using seek(), like magnetic disk
Choose sequential for: log files, streaming data, simple text files where you process从头到尾
Choose direct for: database systems, file editors, random access to large structured files
Direct access requires lseek() system call to position file pointer

questions.question: Describe the structure of the open file table in an operating system. Explain why two levels (system-wide and per-process) are needed.
keyPoints: Two-level table: System-wide table entry shared by all processes + Per-process table entry per process
System-wide table stores: file offset, access mode, flags, pointer to i-node
Per-process table stores: file descriptor, file pointer to system entry
Two levels needed because: file position is per-process (each process has own position in same file), but file itself (inode) is system-wide shared
Enables: independent file positions for forked processes, proper sharing semantics, efficient resource management

questions.question: What are file attributes? List and explain at least five important file attributes stored by modern operating systems.
keyPoints: File attributes/metadata describe a file, not its content
Name: identifier used by users and programs
Type: indicates file format (regular, directory, device, etc.)
Size: current file length in bytes
Permissions: who can read/write/execute (owner, group, others)
Timestamps: creation time, modification time, access time
Location: pointer to disk blocks (via inode)
Owner/Group: user and group IDs for protection

questions.question: Explain the purpose of file buffering in operating systems. How does it improve performance, and what are potential disadvantages?
keyPoints: Buffering temporarily stores data in memory during I/O operations
Purpose: reduces number of physical disk I/O operations by accumulating data
How it works: writes go to buffer first, actual disk write happens when buffer full or file closed
Improves performance: bulk transfers are faster than many small I/O operations
Disadvantages: data loss if system crashes before buffer flushed; delayed writes; complexity in maintaining consistency
Related to caching: caching keeps frequently accessed data in memory for fast retrieval

questions.question: What is the purpose of file locks in operating systems? Explain the difference between shared locks and exclusive locks.
keyPoints: File locks coordinate concurrent access to files by multiple processes
Prevent race conditions and ensure data consistency
Shared Lock (Read Lock): multiple processes can hold simultaneously; allows reading only
Exclusive Lock (Write Lock): only one process can hold; prevents both reads and writes
Can be advisory (processes cooperate) or mandatory (OS enforces)
Examples: flock(), fcntl() in Unix; LockFile() in Windows

questions.question: Differentiate between absolute path names and relative path names in file systems. Give examples and explain when each is appropriate.
keyPoints: Absolute path: starts from root directory, complete path from / (Unix) or drive letter (Windows)
Relative path: interpreted from current working directory
Absolute example: /home/user/documents/report.txt or C:\Users\Admin\file.txt
Relative example: ./docs/notes.txt or ../project/main.c
When to use absolute: when exact location known, scripts, configuration files
When to use relative: within project directories, portable applications, when current directory context matters
cd command changes relative path interpretation

questions.question: Describe what happens internally when a process calls the open() system call to open a file. What data structures are updated?
keyPoints: Kernel checks file existence and permissions
Allocates a file descriptor from process's file descriptor table
Creates/updates entry in system-wide open file table
File pointer initialized to 0 (or end of file for O_APPEND)
Returns file descriptor to process
If O_CREAT and file doesn't exist, creates new file
If error (no permission, file not found), returns -1
Data structures updated: per-process FD table + system-wide open file table + inode reference count incremented

questions.question: A process opens a file using open("data.txt", O_RDWR | O_CREAT, 0644) and gets file descriptor 3. Then it performs: lseek(3, 100, SEEK_SET); read(3, buffer, 50); write(3, buffer, 30); close(3); Explain in detail what happens at each step, including the state of file descriptors, file position pointer, and any system tables involved.
keyPoints: open() with O_RDWR|O_CREAT: Creates file if not exists, opens for read-write, returns fd=3, position=0
lseek(3, 100, SEEK_SET): Sets file position pointer to offset 100 from start
read(3, buffer, 50): Reads 50 bytes starting at offset 100, advances position to 150, data copied to buffer
write(3, buffer, 30): Writes 30 bytes from buffer at current position (150), advances position to 180; may extend file if needed
close(3): Releases file descriptor 3, removes entry from per-process table, decrements system table reference count; if last reference, flushes buffers to disk
System tables: Per-process table entry 3 points to system-wide entry; system entry has offset 180, mode RDWR, pointer to inode
If another process had same file open, its position would be independent

questions.question: Discuss the various file protection mechanisms provided by operating systems. How do they prevent unauthorized access? Explain with examples how permissions work in typical UNIX/Linux systems.
keyPoints: File protection mechanisms: Passwords (not common), Access Control Lists (ACLs), UNIX permissions
UNIX permissions: Owner/Group/Others classification with Read/Write/Execute permissions
Permission bits: 9 bits (rwxr-xr-x) representing owner(3), group(3), others(3)
read (r): view file contents; write (w): modify; execute (x): run program
Directory permissions: r=list files, w=add/remove files, x=access (cd into) directory
chmod command changes permissions (e.g., chmod 755 file)
Also: special bits - SUID, SGID, Sticky bit for additional control
ACLs provide finer-grained control: specific users/groups with specific permissions
Kernel checks permissions on every open() call against process's UID/GID
Superuser (root) bypasses most permission checks

questions.question: Consider a scenario where multiple processes need to access the same file concurrently. Discuss the potential problems that can arise and the solutions provided by the operating system to ensure correct and efficient file access.
keyPoints: Problems: Race conditions, data inconsistency, lost updates when multiple processes write
Process A reads 100 bytes, Process B reads same 100 bytes simultaneously, both modify, both write back - last write wins, first lost (lost update problem)
File locking solutions: Advisory locks (processes voluntarily check locks), Mandatory locks (OS enforces)
Types: Shared locks (multiple readers), Exclusive locks (single writer)
Atomic operations: O_APPEND flag ensures writes go to end atomically
flock() for whole-file locks, fcntl() for record-level locking
fcntl() with F_SETLKW blocks until lock available (wait), F_SETLK returns error if blocked (non-wait)
Lock inheritance: locks are NOT inherited across fork() but CAN be inherited across exec()
Deadlock possibility: if processes hold different locks and wait for each other's locks
Modern solutions: Memory-mapped files (mmap), file leases, mandatory byte-range locks
